California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
SepteDber 23, L99L
ceorge rindall
city of Garden Grove
113 91 Acacia Parkway
P. O. Box 3070
carden Grove, CA 92642
Re: Your Request for Advice
our FiIe No. A-91-414
Dear ltr. Tindall:
rhis is in response to your letter reguesting advice on
behalf of Garden Grove City Councilnember and DeDber of the Garden
crove Agency for conmunity Redevelopnent, J. Tilnan Witliansl
regarding his responsibi I ities under the conf I ict-of- interest
pi5"i"i""= 
of the Political Reforu Act (the "Act").2
OUESTTON
May counciLnenber willians participate in decisions
concerning a proposed anendment of the city redevelopment plan to
include additlonal territory if the council:nernber has multiple
property interests in and around the redeveloprnent area?
CONCLUS ION
since councilmember williams has multiple property interests
inside and within 30o feet of the redeveloprnent area,
councitmenber williarns may not participate in any of the decisions
relating to the arnendment of the redeveloprnent project area.
FACTS
councilmember J. Tilnan williams is a member of the Garden
Grove City council and a member of the Garden Grove Agency for
1 You confirrned in our telephone conversation of sePtember 19,
1.991, that the letter requesting advice was subrnitted at
councilmember willians' request.
2 co.r"r.,..nt code sections 81ooo-91015. Al.I statutory references
are to the covernnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804-0807 . (916) 3 22-5660


Our File No. A-91-4■4
Page 2
Community RedevelopDent ( rrRDA ) ' 
The city council and RDA are
currentty considering anending the redevelopment project to
inctude new territory 
spread throughout the jurj.sdiction. 
In
addition the agencies rrill be considering adding nei{ projects to
the list of public improvenents planned for the redevelopment area
and revising the financial terns of the redevelopment plan.
Specificatly you anticipate the following questions to cone before
the councilmember's agencies:
1. The city council will consider the adoption of a
resolution calling for a joint public hearing on the proposed
redevelopruent plan anendnent and the final environnental inpact
report (the ItEIRir) for such plan amendnent -
2. The city council will consider the adoption of a
resolution to authorize the use of RDA's low and rooderate incorne
housing funds outside of the amended project area.
3. The city council will consider the adoption of a
resolution and ordinance approving the redevelopment plan
anendment, and adoption of a resolution certifying the final ErR'
4. The RDA nill discuss nhat territory 
is to be included in
the anended project area, vith separate discussion for specific
areas (the nirbor Boulevard Properties, the Garden Grove Boulevard
Properties, the westminster Boulevard Properties, and the other
scattered ProPerties) .
5. The RDA will consider the adoption of a resolution to
establish the Iast equalized real proPerty assessnent roll as the
base year for areas to be added to the project area, and
authoiization of transnittal of documents to affected taxing
agencies.
6. The RDA will consider the adoption of a resoluti.on
approving a preliminary report for the ProPosed redevelopment plan
alnendmeni and preliminary report and draft redeveloprnent plan to
affected taxinq agencies, county officials 
and the city planning
commission.
7. The RDA witl hold a public hearing on the draft EIR to
receive public cotTllnent.
8. The RDA will consider the adoption of a resolution
calling for a joint public hearing on the proposed redevelopment
plan amendment and the final EfR, and adoption of a resolution to
authorize the use of the Agency's low and moderate income housing
fund outside of the anended redevelopnent project area.


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-4■4
Page 3
9. The RDA will consider the adoption of a resolution
approving Aqency responses to the report of the fiscal review
colorittee and authorizing transrnittal to the chair of the fiscal
review conmittee.
10. The RDA will discuss the apProval of the RDA report to
the city council on the redeveLopment plan amendment and
authorizing the transuission of such docunent to the city council.
11. The RDA will consider the adoption of a resolution
approving the redevelopnent plan anendment and certifying the
final EIR.
You hav6 asked whether Councilmenber Wiltians may participate
in these decisions before either the city council or RDA. You
stated that counci lmember willians has numerous economic interests
in and around the redevelopnent project area.
1. Counci l:nember Williarns is the lOOt owner of Satellite
Financial, a real estate and insurance brokerage business that is
located in the redevelopnent area.
2. Satellite Financial receives cournission incone fron sales
of real property and insurance fron persons in the existing
project area and in the area to be added-
3. councilneBber WiIliarns also owns the property and
building that Satellite Financial uses as its office in the
existing project area.
4. councilmember Wil.liams owns three other pieces of real
property Iocated in the existing project area which are leased to
tusinesses and generate income.
5. Councilmember Williarns owns t14,o pieces of real Property
located outside, but adjacent to, the existing redevelopment area
and within 3oO feet of territory to be added to the project area.
These properties are leased to residential users.
6. councilmenber willians owns his personal residence which
is approxirnately 1Oo feet fron the existingl project area.
ANALYS I S
The Political Reforn Act (the 'Act"), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The
purpose for the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act is to
ensure that public officials, 
vhether elected or appointed, wi1J.
perform their duties in an inpartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons lrho have supported then. (Section 81001(b).)


Our File No. A-9■-4■4
Page 4
In furtherance of this goal, section 871oo of the Act
prohibits any public official 
frou making, participating in
iaking, or othertrise using his or her official 
position to
influence a governnental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
A rrpublic official" 
is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a
rnember, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
qovernment aqency. This definition would incl'ude members of both
fhe carden Grove city Council and the Garden Grove Agency for
community RedeveloPment.
Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest nithin the neaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that ttre decision wilt have a rnaterial financial
effect, distinguishable fro:n the effect on the public generally,
on the official 
or a menber of his or her irnmediate family or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
officiat 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars (S1,o00) or nore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore.
(c) Any source of incorne, other than gifts
and other than loans by a connercial lendi-ng
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or Inore in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the publ j-c official
within 12 nonths prior to the time when the
decision is rnade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of rnanagement.
Section 87103 (a) - (d).
According to your facts, councilnember williams has a variety
of econornic interests which rnay be foreseeably and naterially
affected by the decisions of these agencies. First, Councilrnember
williams is the loot olrner of satellite Financial, a real estate
and insurance firm which receives cornmission incone fron persons
in the project area.3 ltoreover, the councilnenber or,rns the
property on which his business is situated. 
The business and
3 section
includes a
which the
10 -percent
82030(a)
pro―rata
individual
interest
provides that the j.ncome of
share of any income of any
owns directly, indirectly
or greater.
an individual also
business entity in
or beneficially, a


Our File No. A-9■
-4■4
Page 5
property are located within the redevelopment project area.
The councilnember also o\,rns three other parcels in the
redevelopment project area, all of which are leased for business
purposes. Thus, in addition to the property interests themselves,
the councilmenber receives incoue fron the tenants. Finally, the
councilmernber owns three parcels within 300 feet of the
redevelopnent area, tl{o are rented out for residential purposes,
and one is used as his principal residence. The councilmember
aLso receives income frou the two residential tenants. Each of
the pieces of real property the councilDerlber owns and each source
of incorne to the councilDember is a Potentially disgualifying
economic interest. 
where any one econonic interest of the
councilnembei will be foreseeably and rnaterially affected by a
decision, the councilmenber may not particiPate in the decision.
whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the tiroe a governnental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
coirsidered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. certainty is not reguired.
Holrever, if an effect is only a Eere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. 
( Downev cares v. Downev Cornrnunitv
Development Cornrnission (1987) 196 cal.ApP.3d 938; In re Thorner
(19?5) 1 FPPC oPs. 198.)
The anticipated result of any redeveloprnent project is to
increase the property values and improve the business climate
within and near the redevelopnent project area. The very nature
of redevelopnent projects has Ied the Conmission to find that it
is reasonably foreseeable that there wiIl be a financial effect on
real property values and business interests located within or near
project areas. (In re ooelsbv {1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71; Armento
Advice Letter, No. A-9 0-499. )
However, the councitnernber is only required to disqualify
himself fron the decision if the foreseeable effect on the
counci Imerrber' s economic interests is also material. 
The
Commission has adopted a series of regulations which provide
guidance concerning whether the foreseeabl.e financial effects of a
decision are rnaterial. 
(Regulation L8702. ) These regulations
apply different standards depending on shether the decision wiIl
directly or indirectly affect the official's 
economic interest.
Regulation La702.1(a) (3) (D) applies specifically to
redevelopnent decisions. The regulation provides that a
decision's effect on an official's 
real property is deerned to be
material and disqual if ication is reguired if:
(D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
prelininary p1an, to forn a project area conrnittee,


Our File NO. A-9■-414
Page 6
to certify the environmental docunent, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territorv to the
redeveloornent area, or to rescind or anend any of
the above decisionqi and real oropertv in which the
official 
has an interest, or anv oart of it is
Iocated within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redeveloonent area.
Regulation ta702.1(a) (3) (D),
emphasis added.
Since the councilnenber o$rrls three parcels within the
redeveloprnent area, Regulation La702.1(a) (3) (D) provides the
effect of a clecision on his real property is rnaterial;'
consequently, councilmember Williams nay not participate in the
decision to amend the redevelop:nent project area.
SeЧ =uentation of Dec■s■on
You have asked whether the various other decisions relating
to the amendment of the redeveloproent project area are severable
fron the actual anendnent decision for which the councilnernber is
disquatified. 
Generally, each decision hust be analyzed
independently to deterrnine if there will be a foreseeable rnaterial
financial effect on an official's 
financial interest. 
(fn re olren
(L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) For exanPl.e' lre have advised that
decisions to implement the redevelopment plan after it has been
adopted may be analYzed separately for conf I ict-of- interest
purposes. (Rosenthal Advice Letter, No. I-89-406.)
Moreover, in sorne cases, J.arge and complex decisions may be
divided into separate decisions so that an official 
who is
disqualified as to one component nay stiII participate in
considering other components, provided, of course, that the
decisions are not interrelated and that the official 
has no
independent disqualifying interest with respect to the other
components. (Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)
However, under sone circurnstances a series of decisions may
be too interrelated to be considered separately. For example, if
the resolution of one decision wilL effectively determine the
result of the other decision, the councilmember must disqualify as
to both. This vould be true, for example, if the same policies
and interests are at stake in both decisions. The councilmember
must also disqualify hirnself frorn participating in a1I decisions
l- we have not gone on to analyze the nateriality of the
decision,s effect on the counciLnenber,s other interests since a
foreseeable and rnaterial financial effect on his properties in the
project area are sufficient to result in disgualification.


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-4■4
Page 7
which are not legally separable fron the decision in which he has
a conflict of interest. 
This would be the case, for exauple, if
there were different results in the tr.ro decisions and a legal
challenge would successfully conpel the city council to arrive at
a consistent result. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038; SeC--e-l-E-e,
!{il-Ier Advice Letter, No. A-82-119. )
It appears froD you facts that all the decisions you
discussed are interrelated to the adoPtion of the redeveloPnent
plan amendment. It does not appear that argunents concerning one
of the decisions can be separated fron arguments concerning the
adoption of the arnendrnent. Further, many appear to be lega).Iy
required prerequisites to the amendnent. Therefore, we lrould
conclude, ba5ed upon the infornation provided, that the decisions
set forth in yo-ur letter are not separable fron the redeveloprnent
plan amendrnent.5
"PubIic Generallvrr ExceDtion
PubIic officials with financial interests that wiII be
naterially affected by a decision nay participate in the decision
if the effect on their proPerty is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public aenerally. For the "public AeneraIly'r
exception to apply, a decision must affect the official's
interests in substantiallv the sarne manner as it would affect a
siqnificant seЧ ‖:ent of the public. (Regulation ■8703.)
The 'tpublic'r consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in question. (In re olren (L976t 2 FPPC ops. 77.) This is
so because all the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. 
Thus, the councilnember may participate in the
redevelopnent decisions only if the effect on the interests of the
councilmember is substantially the sane as the effect on a
siqnificant seqment of the population of the city of carden crove.
we do not have information on the size of the redevelopnent
project area in relation to the city as a who1e. However, since
the councilnember has interests in 6 parcels in or r.rithin 3OO feet
of the redevelopment project area, only persons owning
approximately the same amount of property in and near the project
area would be similarly affected. (fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops.
1.) Since, it does not appear that a significant segment of the
population of carden Grove will be affected in substantially the
sane manner as the councilmember, the "public generaLly" exception
would not appfy.
5 wh"." other future decisions concerning the redevelopment
project are separable, the procedure in the Huffaker advice
Letter , S.lJEf!! r should be used . 
We have enc losed a copy of the
Letter for your information.

Page 8

adopted may be analYzed separately for conf I ict-of- interest
purposes. (Rosenthal Advice Letter, No. I-89-406.)
Moreover, in sorne cases, J.arge and complex decisions may be
divided into separate decisions so that an official 
who is
disqualified as to one component nay stiII participate in
considering other components, provided, of course, that the
decisions are not interrelated and that the official 
has no
independent disqualifying interest with respect to the other
components. (Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)
However, under sone circurnstances a series of decisions may
be too interrelated to be considered separately. For example, if
the resolution of one decision wilL effectively determine the
result of the other decision, the councilmember must disqualify as
to both. This vould be true, for example, if the same policies
and interests are at stake in both decisions. The councilmember
must also disqualify hirnself frorn participating in a1I decisions
l- we have not gone on to analyze the nateriality of the
decision,s effect on the counciLnenber,s other interests since a
foreseeable and rnaterial financial effect on his properties in the
project area are sufficient to result in disgualification.

which are not legally separable fron the decision in which he has
a conflict of interest. 
This would be the case, for exauple, if
there were different results in the tr.ro decisions and a legal
challenge would successfully conpel the city council to arrive at
a consistent result. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038; SeC--e-l-E-e,
!{il-Ier Advice Letter, No. A-82-119. )
It appears froD you facts that all the decisions you
discussed are interrelated to the adoPtion of the redeveloPnent
plan amendment. It does not appear that argunents concerning one
of the decisions can be separated fron arguments concerning the
adoption of the arnendrnent. Further, many appear to be lega).Iy
required prerequisites to the amendnent. Therefore, we lrould
conclude, ba5ed upon the infornation provided, that the decisions
set forth in yo-ur letter are not separable fron the redeveloprnent
plan amendrnent.5
"PubIic Generallvrr ExceDtion
PubIic officials with financial interests that wiII be
naterially affected by a decision nay participate in the decision
if the effect on their proPerty is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public aenerally. For the "public AeneraIly'r
exception to apply, a decision must affect the official's
interests in substantiallv the sarne manner as it would affect a
siqnificant seЧ ‖:ent of the public. (Regulation ■8703.)
The 'tpublic'r consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in question. (In re olren (L976t 2 FPPC ops. 77.) This is
so because all the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. 
Thus, the councilnember may participate in the
redevelopnent decisions only if the effect on the interests of the
councilmember is substantially the sane as the effect on a
siqnificant seqment of the population of the city of carden crove.
we do not have information on the size of the redevelopnent
project area in relation to the city as a who1e. However, since
the councilnember has interests in 6 parcels in or r.rithin 3OO feet
of the redevelopment project area, only persons owning
approximately the same amount of property in and near the project
area would be similarly affected. (fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops.
1.) Since, it does not appear that a significant segment of the
population of carden Grove will be affected in substantially the
sane manner as the councilmember, the "public generaLly" exception
would not appfy.
